relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The Jordan miracle becomes fear in the hearts of local kings.
Before battle, Israel's own body is marked again by covenant.
Flint knives turn a hill into a place of irreversible bodily memory.
The rite is explained through the death of the Exodus generation.
Those born in the wilderness had not received the mark.
Forty years of wandering close around the generation that refused the land.
Their children carry covenant into the next crossing.
The camp pauses while wounded bodies recover.
Gilgal names the rolling away of an old reproach.
Passover is kept in the plains of Jericho at the threshold of conquest.
The people eat unleavened bread and roasted grain from the land.
Manna ends when the people can eat from settled earth.
Joshua meets an armed figure and asks the question every army asks: whose side are you on?
The answer is no—or neither—and Joshua is placed beneath a command beyond his faction.
The drawn sword does not make Joshua sovereign; holy ground makes him remove his sandal.

contemplative_note: Joshua 5 refuses a clean triumphal reading. Local people are terrified, Israel is physically vulnerable, manna ends, and the heavenly commander will not simply say 'I am on your side.' Holiness interrupts partisanship before battle begins.
